food_name: Lor Mee (Hokkien Braised Dark Gravy Noodles)
region_of_origin: Singapore (Hokkien and Teochew Chinese immigrant community)
food_subgroup: Braised noodle dish — thick soft yellow flat noodles in cornstarch-thickened dark soy braising gravy with pork belly, fish cake, and braised egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Lor Mee as a prepared Singapore hawker dish. Closest parent would be a generic noodle dish concept; not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — closest parent code used; Lor Mee is a Singapore-specific braised noodle dish not individually catalogued in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for Singapore Lor Mee.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Lor Mee as a prepared hawker dish.

main_ingredients: dark soy braising gravy (dark soy sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, cornstarch)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Lor Mee weighs approximately 460g, comprising thick yellow flat noodles (~150g cooked), cornstarch-thickened dark soy braising gravy (~120ml), braised pork belly slice (~50g), fish cake two slices (~30g), half a braised egg (~25g), bean sprouts (~70g), and fried shallots (~15g).

nutrient_source.title: Lor Mee — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lor Mee combined with component-level calculation for pork belly, fish cake, braised egg, bean sprouts, and dark soy gravy. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. All per-100g values are weighted composites of the dish components at the stated portion ratios.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Noodle Dishes — Analogous Estimate: Thick Soft Wheat Noodles + Gelatinised Cornstarch Gravy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate basis)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Lor Mee. GI ~75 is estimated from two analogous components: (1) thick soft wheat noodles GI ~70 (University of Sydney GI Database, boiled wheat noodles), and (2) fully gelatinised cornstarch-thickened gravy GI ~95 (University of Sydney GI Database, cornstarch). The weighted composite of noodles (~55% of carbohydrate load) and gelatinised starch gravy (~45%) yields an estimated GI of ~75. Confidence 0.9. GL per plate = GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 75 × 68.08 / 100 ≈ 48.75. Carbohydrate per serving = 14.8 g/100g × 460g = 68.08g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium — approximately 1,200mg per plate (260.9mg/100g × 460g), representing ~60% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Lor Mee to occasional consumption (no more than once per week) and request reduced gravy to lower sodium intake; the dark soy braising gravy contributes the majority (~700mg) of the sodium load from the combination of dark soy sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~75) and very high glycemic load (~49 per plate) driven by thick soft wheat noodles and fully gelatinised cornstarch-thickened gravy, making this one of the highest GI/GL Singapore hawker noodle dishes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of Lor Mee due to its very high GL (~49 per plate); if consumed, portion size should be reduced by at least half, vinegar condiment should be added (modestly lowers postprandial glucose response by ~5%), and the dish should be paired with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1,200mg per plate), moderate saturated fat (~7.8g per plate from pork belly), and cholesterol (~129mg per plate) combine to create an unfavourable cardiovascular profile, particularly for patients with existing hypertension or dyslipidaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Lor Mee to occasional consumption due to its high sodium and saturated fat content; requesting less pork belly and reduced gravy can meaningfully lower both sodium and saturated fat per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium (~1,200mg per plate) and very high glycemic load (~49) promote systemic inflammation and weight gain via the insulin pathway, both of which are unfavourable for rheumatoid arthritis management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should avoid regular consumption of Lor Mee; occasional consumption (monthly) is acceptable with extra vinegar to modestly reduce GL, but the high sodium and high GL profile make this dish unsuitable for frequent inclusion in an anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lor Mee (卤面, Loh Mee) is a uniquely Singaporean hawker noodle dish originating from Hokkien and Teochew Chinese immigrant communities. It consists of thick, soft yellow flat wheat noodles served in a generous pool of cornstarch-thickened dark soy braising gravy, topped with braised pork belly slices, fish cake, half a braised egg, bean sprouts, and fried shallots. The gravy is the defining element — deeply savoury, dark brown, glossy, and viscous from fully gelatinised cornstarch, with flavour derived from dark soy sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and five-spice. Vinegar and chilli are traditional accompaniments. One standard plate weighs approximately 460g and provides ~590 kcal, 22g protein, 21g fat, 68g carbohydrate, and approximately 1,200mg sodium. Nutritionally, Lor Mee is one of the highest GI/GL Singapore hawker dishes: GI ~75 (high), GL ~49 per plate (very high), driven by the combination of soft wheat noodles (GI ~70) and fully gelatinised cornstarch gravy (GI ~95). The very high sodium content (60% of WHO daily limit per plate) and high GL make this dish a significant concern for patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork) and is dairy-free. Famous stalls include Bedok North Road Lor Mee, Old Airport Road Food Centre, and Whampoa Drive Makan Place. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes, supplemented by component-level calculation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods (thick soft wheat noodles and gelatinised cornstarch) with documented estimation basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from the verified HPB source record.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Lor Mee as a composite hawker dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source). GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~75) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thick soft wheat noodles (GI ~70) and gelatinised cornstarch (GI ~95); no direct GI measurement for Singapore Lor Mee exists in the published literature as of 2026-04-14. GL per plate = 75 × 68.08 / 100 = 48.75 (carbohydrate per 460g serving = 68.08g). Dish is NOT halal — contains pork belly. Vinegar accompaniment modestly lowers postprandial glycemic response by approximately 5%. Cultural note: Lor Mee is a uniquely Singapore hawker dish not commonly found in mainland China; it is distinct from Taiwanese Lu Rou Fan (braised pork rice) and Malaysian Loh Mee (which uses a different egg-thickened gravy).
